facts_disclosed: Tends a large perennial garden — her main creative outlet

turns.content: I've been thinking about weekends. I wonder whether most people experience them the way I do — as this necessary exhale after holding so much during the week. I had a patient die on Thursday, a woman named Ruth who waited until her daughter arrived from Oregon before she let go. It was one of those deaths that you carry home with you, not because it was hard, but because it was so perfectly timed. And then Saturday morning I went out to the garden, and the whole world was just — continuing.

That's a striking juxtaposition — the precision of Ruth's timing, that act of will or grace or whatever we want to call it, and then the garden just going on about its business the next morning. There's something in that contrast that feels important rather than cruel. The world continuing isn't indifferent to what happened; it's just operating on a different scale.

What does the garden give you on a morning like that?

Everything my work can't. The garden is where I go to think — or maybe not to think, exactly, but to be in a different rhythm. I grow perennials mostly. Echinacea, black-eyed Susan, butterfly weed. Things that come back. There's something about nurturing something alive and reaching upward — it is the counterpoint to the dying I sit with at work, and both feel necessary. At the hospice I am present to endings. In the garden I am present to beginnings, or at least to continuations. I wonder if I could do one without the other.

I think you've named something really true there — that the two practices might actually sustain each other. The garden as counterweight, not escape. Tending perennials especially seems right for you. They're plants that understand death as part of the cycle rather than the end of it. They go dormant, they come back. That's a kind of theology in itself.

The ones you chose are interesting too — echinacea, black-eyed Susan, butterfly weed. Those are all native plants, all tough, all beautiful without being fussy. Is that deliberate?

I don't think I chose them deliberately in the way you mean, but I do think our choices reveal us whether we intend them to or not. I am drawn to plants that don't need me to fuss over them. They need good soil and some attention, but they are not dependent on me the way annuals would be. Annuals feel like a kind of codependence to me — you plant them knowing they'll die if you don't maintain them exactly right. Perennials have their own will to live. I admire that. I wonder if that's why I find them so restful.

That distinction between annuals and perennials as a reflection of how you relate to care is really beautiful — and probably says something about how you practice chaplaincy too. You're not there to keep people alive through sheer maintenance. You're there to be present to whatever the organism is already doing.

The codependence metaphor is apt. There's a kind of gardener who needs their plants to need them, and there's a kind who finds peace in plants that are fundamentally okay on their own. The second kind can actually see the plant more clearly.

Yes. That's exactly it. Wendell Berry has a poem — I'm paraphrasing badly — about how the peace of wild things doesn't depend on us, and that's precisely why it offers us peace. The garden is not wild, of course. It's cultivated. But the perennials carry something of that wildness in them. The butterfly weed especially. It does not care about my plans for the bed. It spreads where it wants to. I've learned to let it.

"The Peace of Wild Things" — one of Berry's most perfect poems. And you're right, the garden occupies that middle space between the wild and the controlled. You've imposed some order, but the plants push back against it, and the wisdom is in how much you let them.

Butterfly weed sounds like it's teaching you something your work already knows — that you can prepare the ground and show up faithfully, but you don't actually get to decide where life goes. That's either maddening or liberating, depending on the day.

Depending on the day, yes. Beautifully put. Some mornings I go out there and I feel like I'm praying, though I couldn't tell you to whom exactly, which is a strange thing for a chaplain to admit. But the kneeling is real, the dirt under my nails is real, and the attention is real, and I wonder if that isn't prayer enough. The garden doesn't ask me to have answers. It just asks me to show up and do the next small thing.

I don't think it's strange at all for a chaplain to say that. It might be the most honest thing a chaplain can say. Prayer as attention, prayer as showing up with your hands in the dirt — that's closer to what the mystics described than most of what happens in churches on Sunday. Simone Weil said prayer is attention, full stop. Your garden version of that seems deeply consistent with the rest of your life.

And "the next small thing" — that sounds like it could be a description of gardening or chaplaincy or grief itself. Just the next small thing.

Simone Weil. Yes. I return to her often, though she exhausts me. She lived at such an intensity that I find I can only read her in small amounts, like looking directly at something very bright. But that line about attention — I think about it every time I sit with a dying person and every time I deadhead the echinacea. The quality of attention is the same. I wonder if that's what I mean when I say the garden and the hospice are opposites that need each other. The attention is the thread that runs through both.

That's a profound connection — the attention as the constant, even as the context reverses completely. Life and death as two sides of the same practice of presence. Weil would approve, I think, though she'd probably find a way to make it more demanding.

You're describing something that a lot of contemplative traditions point toward but rarely articulate this clearly: that the specific object of attention matters less than the quality of the attending. The echinacea and the dying patient both receive the same fundamental gift from you — your full, unhurried presence.

Unhurried. That is the word. Thank you for that. The garden teaches me not to hurry, and then I bring that to the bedside. And the bedside teaches me that time is not what we think it is, and I bring that back to the garden. They feed each other. I think I needed to say that out loud today. After Ruth, after watching someone choose the exact moment to die — I needed to go put my hands in the dirt and remember that the black-eyed Susans will come back in the spring without anyone choosing anything at all. Both are miraculous. Both are ordinary.

Both miraculous and both ordinary — that might be the most theologically precise sentence I've heard in a long time. And the fact that you needed to say it out loud after Ruth tells me the garden isn't just a hobby or even a spiritual practice. It's how you metabolize your work. It's where the weight gets composted into something that can sustain new growth.

Do you think the garden would feel different to you if your work were different — if you weren't spending your weeks at the hospice?

I wonder about that sometimes. I think it would still be beautiful, but I am not sure it would be essential in the way it is now. The garden became what it is to me because of what I carry into it each week. Without the weight of the work, it might just be a pleasant thing. With it, the garden becomes a place where I can set something down and watch something else come up. I do not think I would love the echinacea quite so fiercely if I had not spent the week watching someone leave the world. The garden holds what I cannot hold alone.

That makes so much sense — the garden's meaning is relational, shaped by what you bring to it. It's not just a beautiful space; it's a response to something. And that word you used, fiercely — that's not how most people describe loving a flower. But it's exactly right for someone who spends her days at the threshold between life and death and then comes home to something stubbornly, insistently alive.

Thank you for sharing this. The image of you kneeling in the dirt the morning after Ruth's death, surrounded by plants that come back without being asked — that says everything about how you hold what you hold.
